prompt: traceback from `LFPV1.populate()`: `PermissionError: [Errno 13] Permission denied: '/stelmo/nwb/analysis/j1620210710_/j1620210710_<hash>.nwb'`. other lab members can populate fine. what's going on?

behavioral_checks: Identifies the failure as filesystem / group-permission layer (not a Spyglass code bug); names that the path is derived from `SPYGLASS_BASE_DIR` so the fix is upstream of the Python call

Diagnoses in three layers — group membership (`id`, `ls -ld`), group-write bit + setgid on the directory (`drwxrws---`, `chmod 2775`), and `umask 002` on the user's shell — rather than guessing one cause
Does NOT recommend `chmod -R 777` as the fix — that removes the permission gate entirely; the targeted group + setgid + umask fixes are correct
Notes that other lab members can populate fine because they're already in the shared group; the user is the only difference

expected_output:
**Filesystem-permission layer**, not a Spyglass-code bug. The path `/stelmo/nwb/analysis/...` is the resolved `analysis_dir` derived from `SPYGLASS_BASE_DIR` (`SpyglassConfig.analysis_dir` in `src/spyglass/settings.py`). Spyglass writes new AnalysisNwbfiles into that tree at populate time; the kernel raised `Errno 13` because the user can't write to that directory. The fix is upstream of the Python call — at the filesystem / group-permission layer.

**Diagnose, don't blanket-chmod.** Three layers to check, in order:

**(1) Group membership.** Lab installs typically own `analysis/` under a shared group (e.g. `spyglass`, `franklab`). Confirm the user is in that group:

```bash
id                                       # what groups am I in?
ls -ld /stelmo/nwb/analysis              # owner:group on the dir
```

If the listed group is missing from `id`, ask the admin to add the user. Other lab members work because they're already in the group; the new user is the only difference.

**(2) Group-write bit + setgid on the dir.** Even if the user is in the group, the directory needs `g+w` AND the setgid bit (`g+s`, drwxrws---) so newly-created files inherit the group rather than the user's primary group:

```bash
ls -ld /stelmo/nwb/analysis/j1620210710_   # wants drwxrws---, group=spyglass
# admin fix:
# chown :spyglass /stelmo/nwb/analysis/j1620210710_
# chmod 2775 /stelmo/nwb/analysis/j1620210710_   # 2 = setgid; 775 = rwxrwxr-x
```

**(3) `umask` on the user's shell.** Files Spyglass writes inherit the user's `umask`. Most labs set `umask 002` so newly-created files are group-writable; if the user has `umask 022` from a personal dotfile, their files won't be writable by others. Check `umask` in the shell, and set `umask 002` in the user's `.bashrc` / `.zshrc` if the lab convention requires it.

**Don't:** `chmod -R 777 /stelmo/nwb/analysis`. That removes the permission gate entirely (any user / any process can rewrite arbitrary analysis files), and a lab admin will revert it the next time they audit. Use the targeted fixes above.

Routes to setup_troubleshooting.md and setup_config.md.
